annotations.label: Euclidean vector

anchor.snippet: is a geometric object that has magnitude (or length ) and direction

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace 𝕜 n := PiLp 2 (n → 𝕜)` formalizes the standard Euclidean space whose elements are Euclidean vectors.

annotations.label: Vector quantity

anchor.snippet: A vector quantity is a vector-valued physical quantity

annotations.note: No Mathlib formalization of physical vector quantities was found.

annotations.label: Equipollence of bipoints

anchor.snippet: he made equipollent any pair of parallel line segments of the same length and orientation

annotations.note: Grepping for `equipoll`/`bipoint` returns no relevant Mathlib decls.

annotations.label: Bound vector

anchor.snippet: such a condition may be emphasized calling the result a bound vector

annotations.note: No `BoundVector` notion was found; affine spaces give points but no explicit bound-vector definition.

annotations.label: Free vector

anchor.snippet: the particular initial or terminal points are of no importance, the vector is called a free vector

annotations.note: Vectors in Mathlib (e.g. elements of `EuclideanSpace`) are inherently free vectors with no associated base point.

annotations.label: Equipollent arrows (same free vector)

anchor.snippet: represent the same free vector if they have the same magnitude and direction

annotations.note: No formalization of equipollence of geometric arrows was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vector as equivalence class of equipollent bipoints

anchor.snippet: vectors were introduced (during the 19th century) as equivalence classes under equipollence of ordered pairs of points

annotations.note: This historical quotient construction is not present; Mathlib defines vectors via the abstract module/affine-space axioms instead.

annotations.label: Euclidean space via inner product space and group action

anchor.snippet: a Euclidean space E is defined as a set to which is associated an inner product space of finite dimension over the reals

annotations.note: `InnerProductSpace 𝕜 E` together with finite-dimensionality captures this definition; the affine variant is given by `EuclideanAffineSpace`/`AffineSpace`.

annotations.label: Euclidean vector without reference to a Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: a Euclidean vector is an element of a normed vector space of finite dimension over the reals

annotations.note: Captured by `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)`, a finite-dimensional real normed/inner product space.

annotations.label: Every n-dim Euclidean space is isomorphic to standard

anchor.snippet: every Euclidean space of dimension n is isomorphic to the Euclidean space

annotations.note: `stdOrthonormalBasis 𝕜 E` yields `E ≃ₗᵢ[𝕜] EuclideanSpace 𝕜 (Fin (finrank 𝕜 E))` for any finite-dimensional inner product space.

annotations.label: Force as a one-dimensional vector

anchor.snippet: consider a rightward force F of 15 newtons

annotations.note: Physical examples (forces in newtons) are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Velocity as a vector

anchor.snippet: the velocity 5 meters per second upward could be represented by the vector

annotations.note: Velocity as a physical example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bound vector from two Cartesian points

anchor.snippet: the points A = (1, 0, 0) and B = (0, 1, 0) in space determine the bound vector

annotations.note: Affine `-ᵥ` produces a vector from two points, but no decl for this specific Cartesian example is found.

annotations.label: Sum of two free vectors in coordinates

anchor.snippet: the sum of the two (free) vectors (1, 2, 3) and (−2, 0, 4)

annotations.note: Componentwise addition of vectors in `Fin n → ℝ` / `EuclideanSpace` is given by `Pi.add_apply`.

anchors.snippet: the sum of the two (free) vectors (1, 2, 3) and (−2, 0, 4)

annotations.label: Covariant and contravariant vectors

anchor.snippet: The vector is called covariant or contravariant , depending on how the transformation

annotations.note: No covariant/contravariant vector classification by transformation behavior was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equality of vectors

anchor.snippet: Two vectors are said to be equal if they have the same magnitude and direction

annotations.note: Equality of vectors is just propositional equality `Eq` in Lean core.

anchors.snippet: Two vectors are said to be equal if they have the same magnitude and direction

annotations.label: Opposite vectors

anchor.snippet: Two vectors are opposite if they have the same magnitude but opposite direction

annotations.note: Opposite vector is the additive inverse `-v` in any `AddGroup`/module.

annotations.label: Equidirectional, parallel, antiparallel vectors

anchor.snippet: Two vectors are equidirectional (or codirectional ) if they have the same direction but not necessarily the same magnitude

annotations.note: `SameRay` captures codirectionality (equidirectional vectors); antiparallel can be expressed as `SameRay R x (-y)`, but no dedicated `Parallel`/`Antiparallel` predicate is provided.

annotations.label: Vector addition

anchor.snippet: The sum of a and b of two vectors may be defined as

annotations.note: Vector addition is the `Add` operation of the underlying `AddCommGroup` / module.

anchors.snippet: The sum of a and b of two vectors may be defined as

annotations.label: Commutativity and associativity of vector addition

anchor.snippet: One can check geometrically that a + b = b + a and ( a + b ) + c = a + ( b + c )

annotations.note: `add_comm` and `add_assoc` hold in any `AddCommGroup`, hence on `EuclideanSpace`.

annotations.label: Vector subtraction

anchor.snippet: The difference of a and b is

annotations.note: Vector subtraction is the `Sub` operation on the underlying additive group.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication

anchor.snippet: The operation of multiplying a vector by a scalar is called scalar multiplication

annotations.note: Scalar multiplication is the `SMul`/`Module` action, present on any vector space including `EuclideanSpace`.

annotations.label: Distributivity of scalar multiplication over addition

anchor.snippet: Scalar multiplication is distributive over vector addition in the following sense

annotations.note: `smul_add` (and `add_smul`) directly state distributivity of scalar multiplication over vector and scalar addition.

annotations.label: Length / norm of a vector

anchor.snippet: The length , magnitude or norm of the vector a is denoted by

annotations.note: The norm `‖·‖` from `NormedAddCommGroup`/`EuclideanSpace.norm_eq` formalizes the length of a vector.

annotations.label: Length equals square root of dot product with itself

anchor.snippet: This happens to be equal to the square root of the dot product

annotations.note: `norm_eq_sqrt_re_inner` states `‖x‖ = √(re ⟪x, x⟫)`.

annotations.label: Unit vector

anchor.snippet: A unit vector is any vector with a length of one

annotations.note: Vectors with `‖v‖ = 1` appear pervasively (e.g. `OrthonormalBasis.norm_eq_one`, `Metric.sphere 0 1`) but Mathlib has no standalone `UnitVector` type.

annotations.label: Normalizing a vector

anchor.snippet: To normalize a vector a = ( a 1 , a 2 , a 3 ) , scale the vector by the reciprocal of its length

annotations.note: `NormedSpace.normalize x := ‖x‖⁻¹ • x` exactly matches the article.

annotations.label: Zero vector

anchor.snippet: The zero vector is the vector with length zero

annotations.note: The zero element of an `AddCommGroup`; its norm is zero by `norm_zero`.

annotations.label: Additive identity property of zero vector

anchor.snippet: The sum of the zero vector with any vector a is a

annotations.note: `zero_add a : 0 + a = a` (and `add_zero`) hold in any `AddMonoid`.

annotations.label: Dot product

anchor.snippet: The dot product of two vectors a and b (sometimes called the inner product

annotations.note: The inner product on `EuclideanSpace` (via the `Inner` class / `PiLp.inner_apply`) is exactly the dot product over ℝ.

annotations.label: Dot product in components

anchor.snippet: The dot product can also be defined as the sum of the products of the components of each vector

annotations.note: `PiLp.inner_apply` expresses the Euclidean inner product as the (conjugated) componentwise sum.

annotations.label: Cross product

anchor.snippet: The cross product, denoted a × b , is a vector perpendicular to both a and b

annotations.note: `crossProduct : (Fin 3 → R) →ₗ[R] (Fin 3 → R) →ₗ[R] Fin 3 → R` formalizes the 3D cross product; perpendicularity is `dot_self_cross`/`dot_cross_self`.

annotations.label: Length of cross product is parallelogram area

anchor.snippet: The length of a × b can be interpreted as the area of the parallelogram having a and b as sides

annotations.note: The identity `‖a ⨯₃ b‖ = ‖a‖ * ‖b‖ * sin (angle a b)` is formalized, but Mathlib does not separately define the parallelogram's area to equate with it.

annotations.label: Scalar triple product

anchor.snippet: The scalar triple product is sometimes denoted by ( a b c ) and defined as

annotations.note: `u ⬝ᵥ v ⨯₃ w` is the scalar triple product, with `triple_product_eq_det` and `triple_product_permutation`.

annotations.label: Box product equals parallelepiped volume

anchor.snippet: the absolute value of the box product is the volume of the parallelepiped

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement equating `|u ⬝ᵥ v ⨯₃ w|` with the (Lebesgue) volume of the spanned parallelepiped was found.

annotations.label: Triple product zero iff linearly dependent

anchor.snippet: the scalar triple product is zero if and only if the three vectors are linearly dependent

annotations.note: Combining `triple_product_eq_det` with determinant-linear-dependence characterizations gives the result, but it is not stated explicitly for the triple product.

annotations.label: Triple product positive iff right-handed

anchor.snippet: the box product is positive if and only if the three vectors a , b and c are right-handed

annotations.note: Orientation by sign of det exists abstractly (`Orientation`) but no theorem ties triple-product positivity to right-handedness.

annotations.label: Triple product as 3x3 determinant

anchor.snippet: the scalar triple product is simply the determinant of the 3-by-3 matrix

annotations.note: `triple_product_eq_det : u ⬝ᵥ v ⨯₃ w = Matrix.det ![u, v, w]` is exactly this statement.

anchors.snippet: the scalar triple product is simply the determinant of the 3-by-3 matrix

annotations.label: Triple product multilinear and antisymmetric

anchor.snippet: The scalar triple product is linear in all three entries and anti-symmetric

annotations.note: Multilinearity/alternation follow via `triple_product_eq_det` from determinant properties, but are not stated directly on the triple product.

anchors.snippet: The scalar triple product is linear in all three entries and anti-symmetric

annotations.label: Direction cosine matrix

anchor.snippet: the matrix containing all the c jk is known as the " transformation matrix from e to n "

annotations.note: No `directionCosine`/DCM declaration found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Properties of a direction cosine matrix

anchor.snippet: The properties of a direction cosine matrix, C are

annotations.note: No DCM-specific properties are formalized; orthogonal-matrix facts in `Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.UnitaryGroup` are at a more abstract level.

annotations.label: Seven-dimensional cross product

anchor.snippet: A seven-dimensional cross product is similar to the cross product

annotations.note: Grep for `seven`/`cross7` finds no 7-dimensional cross product in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Position vector

anchor.snippet: can be represented as a position vector whose base point is the origin

annotations.note: A position vector relative to an origin is `p -ᵥ o` in an affine space, but Mathlib has no specific `positionVector` name.

anchors.snippet: can be represented as a position vector whose base point is the origin

annotations.label: Displacement vector

anchor.snippet: their displacement is a vector

annotations.note: `q -ᵥ p` in an `AddTorsor`/`AffineSpace` gives the displacement vector between two points.

annotations.label: Velocity

anchor.snippet: The velocity v of a point or particle is a vector, its length gives the speed

annotations.note: Mathlib has the abstract derivative `deriv`/`fderiv`, but no `Velocity` of a particle trajectory.

anchors.snippet: The velocity v of a point or particle is a vector, its length gives the speed

annotations.label: Acceleration

anchor.snippet: Acceleration a of a point is vector which is the time derivative of velocity

annotations.note: Acceleration as the second time-derivative of position is not formalized as a named concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Work as dot product

anchor.snippet: Work is the dot product of force and displacement

annotations.note: Mechanical work is a physics concept absent from Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Work is the dot product of force and displacement

annotations.label: Contravariant vector via transformation rule

anchor.snippet: A contravariant vector is required to have components that "transform opposite to the basis"

annotations.note: No covariant/contravariant transformation rule formalization was found.

annotations.label: Pseudovector / axial vector

anchor.snippet: A vector which gains a minus sign when the orientation of space changes is called a pseudovector

annotations.note: No `pseudovector`/`axialVector` notion is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angular velocity as pseudovector

anchor.snippet: One example of a pseudovector is angular velocity

annotations.note: Angular velocity as a physical pseudovector example is not formalized.
